content:
16 months of flawless work. A real security hole closed. A board seat, a founding role, an ally inside the kernel. Then one public email settled it.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Nick Fewings on UnsplashFor 16 months, a Microsoft engineer chased a single security feature into the Linux kernel. When the maintainers told him to rewrite it, he rewrote it. When they raised objections, he answered them, one at a time. He won the backing of one of the kernel’s most powerful maintainer teams and drew the open hostility of another.
This was a real production project. More than 800,000 smart network cards in Microsoft’s Azure data centers, a fleet it calls Overlake, run the in-kernel eBPF code he was trying to lock down, and Microsoft’s own security model requires that all code running in the kernel be cryptographically signed. He was closing a real hole in real infrastructure for real reasons. [4]
Sixteen months of correct, patient, responsive work, and by every rule, the project fulfilled itself.
There is only one person on the Linux kernel who has the final say.
He wrote an email about it…
Sixteen months of work, eight hundred thousand machines, and a trillion-dollar…
